data.title: TCW Relative Value Large Cap Fund Q4 2024 Commentary
data.description:
Investment Environment

The U.S. economy continues to show forward momentum. Q3 GDP was revised up to +3.1% due to higher consumer spending and service exports than previously reported. Retail sales (ex. autos) for this year’s shopping season (November 1 through December 24) came at 3.8% y/y per Mastercard SpendingPulse. The ISM PMI rose nearly two points in November from the prior month albeit still below 50. November payrolls number rebounded from October’s hurricane battered reading. The unemployment rate ticked up a notch to 4.2% (still near historical lows) while initial jobless claims remain relatively low. Inflation is still above the Federal Reserve’s (Fed) 2% target. November CPI came in at 2.7% y/y. Much of the November increase was due to stubborn shelter costs. November’s wholesale price index PPI rose 3.0% y/y a bit higher. The Fed’s preferred inflation gauge (Core CPE) for November cooled to 2.8%, its slowest pace since May. Per UBS, third quarter company earnings came in nearly twice expectations, up 9% y/y including buybacks, led in large part to mega IT and Communication Services followed by Health Care, Financials, and Utilities.

The Fed lowered rates by 25 bps at its December meeting but forecasted fewer cuts (two versus four) in 2025 due to inflation concerns. Chair Powell’s hawkish tone triggered a sharp sell-off (and spike in the VIX volatility index; 74%, the second highest single day surge ever) though stocks recouped some of the loss by month end. Mr. Powell acknowledged that inflation has improved but expressed disappointment over the “sideways” movement in recent months with shelter costs not improving as swiftly as hoped. He said, “I think we’re in a good place, but I think from here it’s a new phase and we’re going to be cautious about further cuts.”

Other central banks also cut rates in December. The Bank of Canada cut by 50 bps. In a press release, Governor Macklem said inflation and economic growth no longer require active restraint. Its economy grew by just 1% in 3Q and 4Q looks weaker than projected, and with President-elect Trump threatening a 25% tariff that is a “major source of new uncertainty.” The Swiss National Bank cut by the same magnitude amid depressed inflation and strength in its franc. There is chatter that the central bank may flirt with negative interest rates “if necessary.” The European Central Bank cut by 25 bps though there was some discussion to go with 50 bps and noted that disinflation is “well on track.” President Lagarde signaled that path of rate cuts is downward. The Reserve Bank of Australia made dovish comments but did not cut, the last of the G7 central banks except the Bank of Japan (BoJ) yet to ease.

In order to maintain its 5% GDP goals, the People’s Bank of China has put forth a broad stimulative package to combat slowing growth and waning investor confidence. In November, China announced a $1.4 trillion program (over five years) allowing local governments to refinance debt. More recently, authorities agreed to issue 3 trillion yuan ($411 billion) worth of special treasury bonds in the new year. Recent inflation data in Japan increases the likelihood of a BoJ rate hike(s). The increase was largely due to a winding down of government utility subsidies, something which has prompted Prime Minister Ishiba to reinstate from January to March as part of his economic stimulus package.

Sector Weightings 1

For the fourth quarter, the best performing sectors in the Russell 1000 Value were financials (+7.1%), communication services (+2.4%), and information technology (-0.4%). The bottom performers were materials (-11.5%), health care (-10.4%), and real estate (-6.8%). The portfolio sector’s weights contributed favorably led by the overweight in financials and underweights in health care and materials.

Security Selection 1

Stock selection in the quarter contributed noticeably. The portfolio’s top ten average-weighted names (over the course of the quarter) returned 6.4%, on average, better than the portfolio and benchmark index led by solid gains from Broadcom (AVGO; 4.04%**) and Morgan Stanley (MS; 2.56%**). The portfolio’s information technology and financials stock selection provided the preponderance of the relative outperformance. In IT, the portfolio’s stocks returned 7.7% far ahead of their peers’ -0.4% loss led largely by Broadcom with added support from Salesforce (CRM; 1.74%**) and Flex (FLEX; 1.92%**). In financials, the portfolio’s names’ rose 11.7% versus the group gain of 7.1% highlighted by Apollo Global Management (APO; 1.96%**), Morgan Stanley, and Fiserv (FI; 2.71%**). One additional notable outperformer included McKesson (MCK; 3.04%**).

On the bottom end, the most noticeable laggard was homebuilder Lennar (LEN; 2.75%**). The stock fell after the company posted weaker than expected results and lowered guidance with uncertainty around mortgage rates. Lennar’s underperformance raised a fundamental review flag and within five business days, the position was added to slightly with an eye toward the upcoming spring home buying season. GE Aerospace (GE; 2.92%**) was largely responsible for the underperformance in industrials where the portfolio’s stocks fell -5.8% versus the group return of -2.1%. The portfolio’s consumer staples names declined -13.9% versus their peers’ loss of -2.7% mostly due to Target (TGT; 0.89%**). Its stock fell sharply after reporting both revenues and earnings below analysts’ expectations. On the earnings call, the company’s CEO cited a more discerning consumer whose budgets “remain stretched” and who are “focusing on deals” and “stocking up when they fine them.” Target was reduced on its fundamental review flag. AES’s (AES; 0.64%**) sharp decline was mostly to blame for the decline in utilities though its relatively small weighting softened the blow. Its stock fell after reporting mixed results with earnings beating but revenues falling short. Additionally, AES, along with utilities in general, has come under pressure with more attractive income opportunities with the recent spike in bond interest rates. With the recent strengthening in the U.S. dollar, materials stocks have felt the brunt and portfolio holding Freeport-McMoRan (FCX; 1.14%**) was not immune leading to underperformance relative to their peers. Other notable detractors included health care names Elevance Health (ELV; 0.63%**) and Amgen (AMGN; 1.81%**).

Representative Buys (Ticker, Sector)*

Procter & Gamble (PG; Consumer Staples) (0.51%**)

MSCI ESG Score 2: Overall AA

Procter & Gamble (PG), headquartered in Cincinnati OH, is one of the world’s largest manufacturers of branded consumer packaged goods. PG operates within five segments: 1) Fabric and Home Care, 2) Baby, Feminine and Family Care, 3) Beauty, 4) Grooming, and 5) Healthcare. The company’s leading brands include Tide, Cascade, Crest, Gillette, Vicks, Head & Shoulders, Pantene, Olay, Charmin, Bounty, just to name a few. At initiation, PG had a $400 billion market capitalization and met one (and statistically the most correlative) of five valuation factors, dividend yield of 2.4%. The catalyst is cost cutting and restructuring. The company has a long-term track record of delivering substantial productivity savings with operating margin expansion of nearly 300 bps over the last five years. PG’s management team expects impressive productivity gains of at least $2 billion in FY25 and has a strong pipeline of opportunities over the next few years which should drive strong margin expansion.

UnitedHealthcare Group Inc. (UNH: Health Care) (0.66%**)

MSCI ESG Score: Overall A

UnitedHealthcare Group, headquartered in Minnetonka, MN, is the largest provider of healthcare insurance plans and other related healthcare services in the United States. UNH offers a broad spectrum of network-based managed care health benefit plans to the Medicare, Medicaid, Healthcare Exchanges, government, employee, individual markets. In addition to medical and pharmacy benefits services, the company operates through the following segments: UnitedHealthcare, Optum Rx, Optum Health and Optum Insight. UnitedHealthcare also offer healthcare insurance products for Medicare, Medicaid, Healthcare Exchanges, as well as for commercial employers and individuals. At initiation, UNH had

$568 billion market capitalization and met three of the five valuation factors (price-to-sales, price-to-earnings and dividend yield). The catalysts are new management and new markets/new products. In February 2021, Andrew Witty was appointed as CEO and Director. The company has expanded its home health and behavioral offerings and other medical services. The company is also undertaking a strong program to enhance efficiency and business optimization through technology applied to various aspects of its business from medical utilization to real estate optimization and integrate AI capability across all its business aspects. The successful implementation of these catalysts should lead to improved operating margins, cash flow generation, and increased earnings.

Representative Sells (Ticker Sector)*†

There were no complete sells this quarter.

Market Outlook

Stock prices should continue to follow earnings growth. Corporate earnings are expected to increase in Q4 from their Q3 pace to ~12% with banks poised to be the biggest contributor based on easier compares lapping FDIC special assessments; semiconductors and pharmaceuticals are expected to be #2 and #3. Expectations are for low- to mid-teens earnings growth in 2025. We expect the broad U.S. markets to rise 12-15% in 2025, more should fiscal actions continue to flow to consumers and businesses. The Magnificent 7 Q3 reports demonstrated their slowest collective expansion in six quarters. What’s more, the earnings gap between Big Tech and the rest of the market is expected to continue to narrow into 2025 which should turn the tide in favor of value factors.

Slower growth but no hard landing should be supportive for other companies not already buoyed by AI infrastructure. Additional points for value outperformance in 2025 include lower corporate taxes which should favor currently higher tax paying U.S. domestic sectors such as Financials and Health Care. Also, historically, changes in U.S. presidential regimes change the underlying market dynamics. One example is value outperforming growth in 2016 (17.3% vs. 7.1%) followed by growth outperforming value in 2017 (30.2% v. 13.7%) as represented by the Russell 1000 Value and Growth Indices. We expect regulatory relief will be a positive for Financials, Industrials, and small- to medium-size businesses. There is increased anxiety and potential volatility this year with Trump’s pro-tariff stance and, with it, the possibility for upward pressure on inflation. As part of his first executive orders, Trump has stated that he will impose additional 10% tariffs on Chinese goods and 25% on Mexico and Canada citing “crime and drugs.”

The performance headwind facing the portfolio’s underweights in expensive megacaps could become a tailwind in 2025. We believe the U.S. is a global haven for all asset classes particularly on the back of a strong U.S dollar. Geopolitical risks abound and the Eurozone’s two largest economies, France and Germany, face internal strife. Canada as well is facing slow growth and a government regime change. We are busy and actively working to make the best portfolio decisions possible with the information available and will adjust should the “facts” change. We are constructive about the future but maintain strong risk controls for unexpected turbulent events.

Diane E. Jaffee, CFA, Group Managing Director

Matthew J. Spahn, Managing Director

Past performance does not guarantee future results.

Glossary of Terms

AAA Rating– Rating issued to investment-grade debt that has a high level of creditworthiness with the strongest capacity to repay investors. Artificial Intelligence (AI)– The theory and development of computer systems able to perform tasks that normally require human intelligence, such as visual perception, speech recognition, decision-making, and translation between languages. Basis Points (bps)– A unit that is equal to 1/100th of 1%, and is used to denote the change in a financial instrument. The basis point is commonly used for calculating changes in interest rates, equity indexes and the yield of a fixed-income security. BBB Rating– A BBB rating represents a relatively low-risk bond or investment. BB Rating– Bonds which are less vulnerable to nonpayment than other speculative issues. However, they face major ongoing uncertainties or exposure to adverse business, financial, or economic conditions which could lead to the obligor’s inadequate capacity to meet its financial commitment on the obligation. Benchmark– A standard against which the performance of a security, mutual fund or investment manager can be measured. Generally, broad market and market-segment stock and bond indexes are used for this purpose. Buybacks– The repurchase of outstanding shares by a company in order to reduce the number of shares on the market. Cash Flow– The movement of money into or out of a business, project, or financial product. Consumer Price Index (CPI)– Measures the average change in prices over time that consumers pay for a basket of goods and services. Dividend– A distribution of a portion of a company’s earnings, decided by the board of directors, to a class of its shareholders. Dividend Yield– A financial ratio that shows how much a company pays out in dividends each year relative to its share price. In the absence of any capital gains, the dividend yield is the return on investment for a stock. ECB (European Central Bank)– The European Central Bank (ECB) is the central bank responsible for monetary policy of the European Union (EU) member countries that have adopted the euro currency. This currency union is known as the eurozone and currently includes 19 countries. The ECB’s primary objective is price stability in the euro area. ESG– Environmental, Social, and Governance. Eurozone– A geographic and economic region that consists of all the European Union countries that have fully incorporated the euro as their national currency. Expense Ratio– A measure of what it costs an investment company to operate a mutual fund. Federal Reserve (the Fed)– The central bank of the United States which regulates the U.S. monetary and financial system. GDP (Gross Domestic Product)– The market value of all final goods and services produced within a country in a given period of time. Global Industry Classification Standard (GICS)– A standardized classification system for equities developed jointly by Morgan Stanley Capital International (MSCI) and Standard & Poor’s. Growth– A diversified portfolio of stocks that has capital appreciation as its primary goal, with little or no dividend payouts. Portfolio companies would mainly consist of companies with above-average growth in earnings that reinvest their earnings into expansion, acquisitions, and/or research and development. Hard Landing– An economic state wherein the economy is slowing down sharply or is tipped into outright recession after a period of rapid growth, due to government attempts to rein in inflation Hawkishness– Generally favoring relatively high interest rates in order to keep inflation in check. Inflation– A condition of a rise in the general level of prices of goods and services in an economy over a period of time. U.S. ISM Manufacturing Purchasing Managers Index (PMI)– An indicator of the economic health of the manufacturing sector. The PMI is based on five major indicators: new orders, inventory levels, production, supplier deliveries and the employment environment. Large Capitalization (Large Cap)– Companies with a market capitalization value of more than $10 billion. Margin– In a general business context, the difference between a product’s (or service’s) selling price and the cost of production. Market Capitalization– Represents the aggregate value of a company or stock. It is obtained by multiplying the number of shares outstanding by their current price per share. Mutual Funds– An investment vehicle that is made up of a pool of funds collected from many investors for the purpose of investing in securities such as stocks, bonds, money-market instruments and similar assets. Mutual funds are operated by money managers, who invest the fund’s capital and attempt to produce capital gains and income for the fund’s investors. A mutual fund’s portfolio is structured and maintained to match the investment objectives stated in its prospectus. Overweight– A condition where the portfolio exposure to a given asset class (or risk measure) exceeds that of the benchmark index. PBOC (People’s Bank of China)– China central bank. PPI (Producer Price Index)– A family of indexes that measures the average change in selling prices received by domestic producers of goods and services over time. PPIs measure price change from the perspective of the seller. Price-to-Book (P/BV or Price/Book or P/B)– A ratio used to compare a stock’s market value to its book value. It is calculated by dividing the current closing price of the stock by the latest quarter’s book value per share. Private Equity– An asset class consisting of equity securities and debt in operating companies that are not publicly traded on a stock exchange. Restructuring– A significant modification made to the debt, operations or structure of a company. Standard & Poor’s 500 Index (S&P 500)– An index of 505 stocks issued by 500 large companies with market capitalizations of at least $6.1 billion. It is seen as a leading indicator of U.S. equities and a reflection of the performance of the large-cap universe. Stimulus– Something that causes a reaction; an incentive. Tariff– A tax imposed on imported goods and services. Underperform– When an investment is underperforming, it is not keeping pace with other securities or indices. Unemployment Rate– The percentage of the total labor force that is unemployed but actively seeking employment and willing to work. Value– A fund that primarily holds stocks that are deemed to be undervalued in price and that are likely to pay dividends. VIX– Chicago Board of Exchange (CBOE) Volatility. Volatility– A measure of the risk of price moves for a security calculated from the standard deviation of day-to-day logarithmic historical price changes. Yield– The income return on an investment. This refers to the interest or dividends received from a security and is usually expressed annually as a percentage based on the investment’s cost, its current market value or its face value.

data.title: Total Return Forecasts: Major Asset Classes - April 2, 2025
data.description:
The long-run expected total return for the Global Market Index (GMI) fell again in March, dropping to an annualized 6.9% vs. the previous month’s 7.1%. The analysis is based on three models (defined below) for GMI, a global benchmark that’s based on a market value-weighted mix of the major asset classes (excluding cash).

In line with recent history, US equities are still the downside outlier for expected return relative to the market’s history and the various asset classes that comprise GMI. The average forecast for American shares is still printing well below its trailing 10-year performance. The implication: US shares are expected to earn noticeably softer results in the years ahead versus American equities’ return over the past decade. By contrast, the rest of the major asset classes continue to post return forecasts above their trailing 10-year records. On that basis, the outlook for a globally diversified portfolio looks more attractive compared to the past decade, a point we’ve making for months, based on this data.

GMI represents a theoretical benchmark for the “optimal” portfolio that’s suited for the average investor with an infinite time horizon. Accordingly, GMI is useful as a starting point for customizing asset allocation and portfolio design to match an investor’s expectations, objectives, risk tolerance, etc. GMI’s history suggests that this passive benchmark’s performance is competitive with most active asset-allocation strategies, especially after adjusting for risk, trading costs and taxes.

It’s likely that some, most or possibly all of the forecasts above will be wide of the mark in some degree. GMI’s projections, however, are expected to be somewhat more reliable vs. the estimates for its components. Predictions for the specific markets (US stocks, commodities, etc.) are subject to greater volatility and tracking error compared with aggregating the forecasts into the GMI estimate, a process that may reduce some of the errors through time.

Another way to view the projections above is to use the estimates as a baseline for refining expectations. For instance, the point forecasts above can be adjusted with additional modeling that accounts for other factors not used here - current valuation, such as dividend yield, or momentum, for instance.

For perspective on how GMI’s realized total return has evolved through time, consider the benchmark’s track record on a rolling 10-year annualized basis. The chart below compares GMI’s performance vs. the equivalent for US stocks and US bonds through last month. GMI’s current return for the past ten years is 7.1%, a solid performance, albeit a performance that’s downshifted in recent months and is well below the heights in past years.

Here’s a brief summary of how the forecasts are generated and definitions of the other metrics in the table above:

BB: The Building Block model uses historical returns as a proxy for estimating the future. The sample period used starts in January 1998 (the earliest available date for all the asset classes listed above). The procedure is to calculate the risk premium for each asset class, compute the annualized return and then add an expected risk-free rate to generate a total return forecast. For the expected risk-free rate, we’re using the latest yield on the 10-year Treasury Inflation Protected Security (OTCPK:TIPS). This yield is considered a market estimate of a risk-free, real (inflation-adjusted) return for a “safe” asset - this “risk-free” rate is also used for all the models outlined below. Note that the BB model used here is (loosely) based on a methodology originally outlined by Ibbotson Associates (a division of Morningstar).

EQ: The Equilibrium model reverse engineers expected return by way of risk. Rather than trying to predict return directly, this model relies on the somewhat more reliable framework of using risk metrics to estimate future performance. The process is relatively robust in the sense that forecasting risk is slightly easier than projecting return. The three inputs:

* An estimate of the overall portfolio’s expected market price of risk, defined as the Sharpe ratio, which is the ratio of risk premia to volatility (standard deviation). Note: the “portfolio” here and throughout is defined as GMI

* The expected volatility (standard deviation) of each asset (GMI’s market components)

* The expected correlation for each asset relative to the portfolio (GMI)

This model for estimating equilibrium returns was initially outlined in a 1974 paper by Professor Bill Sharpe. For a summary, see Gary Brinson’s explanation in Chapter 3 of The Portable MBA in Investment. I also review the model in my book Dynamic Asset Allocation. Note that this methodology initially estimates a risk premium and then adds an expected risk-free rate to arrive at total return forecasts. The expected risk-free rate is outlined in BB above.

ADJ: This methodology is identical to the Equilibrium model (EQ) outlined above with one exception: the forecasts are adjusted based on short-term momentum and longer-term mean reversion factors. Momentum is defined as the current price relative to the trailing 12-month moving average. The mean reversion factor is estimated as the current price relative to the trailing 60-month (5-year) moving average. The equilibrium forecasts are adjusted based on current prices relative to the 12-month and 60-month moving averages. If current prices are above (below) the moving averages, the unadjusted risk premia estimates are decreased (increased). The formula for adjustment is simply taking the inverse of the average of the current price to the two moving averages. For example: if an asset class’s current price is 10% above its 12-month moving average and 20% over its 60-month moving average, the unadjusted forecast is reduced by 15% (the average of 10% and 20%). The logic here is that when prices are relatively high vs. recent history, the equilibrium forecasts are reduced. On the flip side, when prices are relatively low vs. recent history, the equilibrium forecasts are increased.

Avg: This column is a simple average of the three forecasts for each row (asset class)

10yr Ret: For perspective on actual returns, this column shows the trailing 10-year annualized total return for the asset classes through the current target month.

Spread: Average-model forecast less trailing 10-year return.

data.title: Elastic: Consistent Performance At A Fantastic Price
data.description:
While volatility is swirling in the stock markets, long-term oriented investors have a great chance to pick up shares of fantastic growth stocks while their share prices are dislocated from fundamentals. While I continue to believe that the overall recession has room on the downside, we should continue to increase our exposure to "growth at a reasonable price" stocks on the way down.

Elastic (NYSE:ESTC), in my view, is a stock that is dramatically underappreciated. The enterprise search company has seen true demand strength from generative AI investments that have led the company to sustain a high-teens growth pace, while at the same time padding its margins. And yet, over the past year, the stock has slid just shy of ~10%, while the recent market downturn has also knocked nearly 25% off Elastic's near-term peaks in February in the mid-$110s: technically putting the stock in bear market territory. In my view, it's a great time for reinvests to reassess the bull case in this stock.

I last wrote a buy opinion on Elastic in November, when the stock was still rallying off a fiscal Q2 beat-and-raise that took the stock briefly to the high $110s. Since then, the stock's valuation has continued to slide down further, while Elastic's performance continues to hold up against a tough macroeconomy. While many of the company's peers in the software sector are reporting higher deal scrutiny as an excuse for slowdown in revenue growth and contracting net expansion rates, Elastic's core growth metrics are staying firm. With this in mind, I'm reiterating my buy opinion on Elastic.

Since valuation is one of the major draws to investing in this name, we'll start with where the stock is trading against the latest expectations. Elastic isn't a deep-value stock: but it's trading at a fair price for the growth it delivers and the uniqueness of its product platform. At current share prices near $91, Elastic trades at a market cap of $9.54 billion. After we net off the $1.28 billion of cash against $569.4 million of debt on Elastic's latest balance sheet, the company's resulting enterprise value is $8.82 billion.

Elastic will close out its FY25 at the end of April, one of the few companies in the tech sector to have an April year-end. For FY26 (the year ending in April 2026), Wall Street analysts are currently expecting Elastic to generate $1.69 billion in revenue, which represents 14% y/y growth. This positions Elastic's valuation at just 5.2x EV/FY26 revenue.

We note that the major bellwethers of the software sector, Salesforce (CRM) and Workday (WDAY), trade at slightly richer ~6x forward revenue multiples despite the fact that their growth has slowed to the mid/low teens. Meanwhile, peers like ServiceNow (NOW) which is expecting slightly higher growth in high teens for the coming fiscal year trades at multiples that are nearly double that of Elastic.

Elastic, in my view, is suffering from a discount due to its lack of recognition among the larger software brands. And yet, the company offers a fantastic search product that forms the backbone of many (most) enterprise applications. As a reminder to investors who are newer to Elastic, the company services a massive $78 billion TAM, and the race to build generative AI applications is already driving more demand for Elastic's tools.

Stay long here and use the recent dip as a buying opportunity.

Q3 download

Let's now go through Elastic's latest fiscal Q3 results in greater detail. The Q3 earnings summary is shown below:

Elastic's revenue grew 17% y/y to $382.1 million, beating Wall Street's expectations of $368.9 million (+12% y/y) by a massive five-point margin. What makes Elastic stand out versus its peers is the fact that the company has been able to maintain relatively stable growth rates in a very tough macroeconomic climate. The chart below showcases that the company has maintained a ~17-18% growth rate in each of the past three quarters. Meanwhile, the company's cloud revenue (now just shy of half of overall revenue) actually saw a slight acceleration in constant-currency growth rates to a staggering 47% y/y.

Also notable is the fact that Elastic's net revenue retention rate has clung consistently to a 112% range, indicating that the average customer is expanding their spend on the Elastic platform by 12%. Net revenue retention rates for the rest of the software sector, meanwhile, have been under pressure. This has been driven by both headcount reduction/hiring slowdowns that have impacted seat-based products, as well as higher IT budget scrutiny that has impeded the process of buying new modules and add-ons.

Elastic's relatively firmer growth rates, meanwhile, are the result of its own stronger sales execution, especially after the company changed its approach to segment its teams better between smaller mid-market customers and larger enterprise prospects. Per CEO Ash Kulkarni's remarks on the Q3 earnings call:

During the quarter, we saw continued progress in our go-to-market motion. At the start of this fiscal year, we implemented field segmentation changes that increased our focus on landing and expanding enterprise and high-potential mid-market customers. These changes resulted in some unanticipated sales execution issues in Q1 that we have since been addressing.

Our performance in Q3 demonstrates that we are now back to the levels of sales execution that we have seen in the past and we are even starting to see the expected positive impact from the earlier segmentation changes. Q3 performance benefited from our maniacal focus on these customer segments and deal flow remained strong during the quarter as we grew commitments from new and existing customers across all of our solutions. To that point, we have already added significantly more customers spending over $1 million with us through the first three quarters of this fiscal year versus all of last year.

Generative AI is enabling organizations to extract value from unstructured data, documents and logs. Search is core to value extraction, and we saw outperformance from search in Q3 with our search business once again accelerating year-over-year.

Elastic has been no slouch in the profitability department, either. As shown in the chart below, pro forma operating margins improved 360bps y/y to 16.8%:

The company attributed the margin gains to disciplined spending, and with the company's recent trend of strong net expansion rates, we expect further operating leverage and margin gains in the coming FY26.

Key takeaways

Strong growth, a large market opportunity, consistent margin expansion, and a modest valuation at ~5x forward revenue - there's a lot to like about Elastic on the recent dip. We'll caution that this is a stock that requires patience as it rebuilds investors' trust in its sales momentum and gains better name recognition, but in my view, this is a safe long-term hold that you can comfortably buy and sit on in this market downturn.

data.title: Quanterix: Still Many, Many Questions
data.description:
When Quanterix (NASDAQ:QTRX) pursued a big deal for Akoya at the start of the year, I wondered why the company made such a move. A lossmaking business was acquiring another lossmaking business, which looked odd at first sight, yet the company anticipates real revenues and cost synergies, pushing forward the path to breakeven.

Investors have some concerns on this, as I struggle to really understand the rationale, a bit as well. With shares trading below net cash holdings, skepticism is high, but the company has some time and cash to deliver on its promises here.

About Quanterix

Quanterix is a life science business that went public back in 2017. The company was built on the belief, albeit a risky one, that it could commercialize its ultra-sensitive digital immunoassay platform, used in precision diagnostics. In essence, this technology requires much lower concentrations of fluids compared to conventional technologies in detection; in fact, it requires a simple molecule for detection purposes.

The business went public at $15, valuing the business at around $300 million, based on $25 million in sales reported from this Simoa technology. The business boomed during the pandemic, as shares rose to a high around the $100 mark on the hopes of a role that the company could play during the pandemic, as shares have fallen back to IPO levels soon thereafter.

In the meantime, the company has grown sales from $25 million in 2017 to $120 million in 2023, yet this growth was continuously accompanied by operating losses which resulted in continued dilution.

2023 marked an important year as real operating leverage was reported upon. Revenues were up 16% to $121 million, as gross margins improved by twelve points to 50% of sales. The company still posted operating losses of $50 million, a huge amount, by all means.

The company guided for 2024 sales to increase by 16-17% to $139-$144 million, with gross margins up to 51-55%, yet this would still result in substantial losses reported, as this was a $20 stock early in 2024.

About 2024

After a reasonable first quarter, the company cut the full-year sales guidance to $134-$138 million alongside the release of the second quarter earnings results, causing shares to fall to the low teens.

With 38 million shares outstanding around the $10 mark, the business was valued at less than $400 million at the start of the year, as expectations were down substantially as the company operated with a substantial net cash position.

The real shocker was that the company announced the purchase of Akoya Biosciences (AKYA) in order to integrate spatial biology capabilities in tissue with ultra-sensitive detection of biomarkers in blood. The deal adds about $80-$85 million in sales, for a pro forma revenue base of around $220 million.

Akoya, however, was posting revenue declines, with realistic operating losses reported around $40 million, as the pro forma results called for sales around $220 million and operating losses of $80-$90 million. This, however, was supposed to be partially offset by $20 million in synergies in 2025 and $40 million in synergies in 2026. Despite the potential synergies, I was puzzled behind the reason for the deal and the willingness to dilute at lower share prices as the deal involved a large stock component.

Coming Down Further

A high single stock early in January has fallen to $6 and change at this moment in time, trading near the lows. By mid-January, shares had traded around the $10 mark as the company posted a 12% increase in full-year preliminary sales to $137 million.

Similar definitive sales numbers were reported in mid-March, as GAAP operating losses increased slightly to $52 million and change. Given the impact of the transaction, investors were looking forward. The own business was set to grow sales by just 2-6% in 2025 with revenues seen up to just $140-$146 million, essentially in line with the original outlook for 2024! Worse, is that gross margins are seen stable at around 55%, indicating another year of substantial losses, as this, of course, comes ahead of the Akoya deal.

With 38 million shares trading at $6 and change, the market value of the firm comes to around a quarter of a billion, actually less than net cash holdings of $289 million, of course, ahead of the Akoya deal, which is set to close in the second quarter.

Final Words

Given the organic path of the business and that of Akoya in recent times, the 2026 outlook which calls for double-digit organic growth and cash flow positive operations looks quite a stretch. That said, with a substantial net cash position of around $175 million seen upon closing, there is real potential if the company can deliver, which is something that investors are clearly doubting.

That said, with a negative operating valuation at this point in time, expectations are low as there is some cash runway and real revenues that can keep the company afloat for some time, and in fact unleash great value to be created over time, if the company delivers on (even part) of its promises.

This is the potential, yet my hopes on this are minimal given the past track record and other capital allocation decisions made.

All this makes it interesting to keep a close eye on the shares from here, as I look forward to more clues on the prospects of the business and shares upon closing of the Akoya deal in the coming months, and/or update on the full-year guidance.

data.title: NIO: Don't Make A Grave Mistake In Buying This Dip
data.description:
Chinese equities have enjoyed a remarkable comeback in 2025 as the Trump Administration sent global investors sprawling for cover, as they try to anticipate and assess what’s next from President Trump, as he gets ready to unveil the next salvo in his tariffs package aimed at “restoring” American manufacturing prowess.

Trump’s “Liberation Day” is set to be announced on the afternoon of April 2, which could see among the highest tariffs rates being unravel in the modern era, which has the potential to drive more investor allocation away from the US market, but could also attract dip-buyers looking for some semblance of normalcy, as we get more clarity over the size and extent of those tariffs.

Hence, it has been a phenomenal start for the China’s EV leader BYD Company (OTCPK:BYDDF), but the same cannot be said of Nio Inc. (NYSE:NIO), as it struggles for market share amid intense competition within the Chinese EV market.

The bifurcation in their performance cannot be further overstated as BYD surges to new highs, while NIO continues languishing at its recent lows. While NIO appears to be hovering over key support zones (potenitally preceding a relief rally), the company continues to face significant challenges in overcoming its negative profitability, complexities in penetrating adjacent markets (with sub-brands), while also facing a dominant BYD as it capitalizes on its vertically integrated value chain to “squeeze” its competitors.

Nio’s delivery updates for Q1 shouldn't have surprised investors since the company has already provided an update at Nio's recent earnings conference in late March. That being said, the guidance was obviously disappointing as Nio investors endured a post earnings sell-off, suggesting the momentum from 2024 might not be repeated this year.

Accordingly, the company delivered about 42K vehicles in Q1, slotting in nicely against its guidance range of between 41K and 43K vehicles. So I think that should provide some assurance to investors that Nio could at least execute accordingly, as it still represents a remarkable 40% YoY increase in deliveries.

However before investors start getting elated with the performance, I don't think it has mitigated the concerns about whether Nio could realistically meet (much less surpass) its delivery performance for 2024? As a reminder, management provided much fodder for thought, when it telegraphed its intent to double its deliveries for 2025 from last year’s metric of 222K units.

A highly ambitious undertaking to say the least. But, for investors aware of my previous articles on Nio, I have also enunciated how the it hasn’t always been on point with its execution, while facing immense complexities in reaching profitability. Therefore, we must pay more attention into how the company could expand its offerings in the other sub-brand segements, like ONVO and Firefly, to help the company scale up more quickly, while contending with a resurgent XPeng (XPEV), while fending off challenges from Li Auto (LI) and not to mention Xiaomi (OTCPK:XIACF), which has made a stellar entry into the EV market, deserving the accolades from its ability to broaden its smartphone ecosystem into EVs.

Still, I must give credit to Nio for finally hitting well above the 220K deliveries waypoint in 2024, although I must also emphasize that we are no longer in 2024. In other words, investors shouldn't just marvel over its remarkable achievement but forget that the market will scrutinize the strategy in pushing for a 440K deliveries objective for 2025.

And with just over 40K vehicles delivered in Q1, that would necessitate the company to deliver 400K vehicles over the next three quarters, an arguably gargantuan effort that is seemingly straightforward for BYD Company, but surely not as easy for Nio. The EV maker must juggle between its necessity for scale while trying to validate its ability to meet its vehicle margins estimate of 20% (for Nio main brand) and 15% for its sub-brands, while calling out a 2M long-term deliveries outlook.

Look, it’s one thing to say that you want to deliver 2M units, and another thing to actually do it. I'm not insinuating that Nio isn’t up to the task. But, if BYD has surged to new highs while NIO continues to languish this year, I believe it clearly shows that investors aren’t convinced with management’s enthusiasm, given what it has delivered for Q1.

Despite that, there’s little doubt that Nio operates in a secular growth industry as EV penetration rate continues to increase, but clearly a 200K deliveries output remains well below what Nio needs to achieve breakeven based on the current outlook.

And the situation is compounded by the possibility that Nio is assessed by Wall Street to burn through another $3B from FY25-26. Based on Q4, Nio reported about $4.6B in cash and equivalents, so the company can still manage through FY2026, but will likely need to start turning profitable from FY2027 to remain sustainable, or investors might need to face the specter of more equity dilution to keep the company going.

Even the recent fund raising that saw Nio raising HKD4.03B through a share placement, but diluted about 6% of its holdings. With Nio’s current market cap at just over $8B, I believe the company is constriained in raising equity more aggressively to scale its R&D investments and capital spending without causing more mayhem on investor sentiments on its execution.

In other words, I believe the next two quarters will have significant ramifications on whether investors can still have sufficient confidence in management’s ability of meeting its 400+K deliveries target, or assess whether Nio could face more impediments, even as the rotation into Chinese equities have benefited BYD and its tech peers.

So tell me what do you see above? unless we are looking at a different chart, I'm pretty sure you see a downtrending stock, isn't it? And these are stocks that I often remind members in my investing group (Ultimate Growth Investing) to clearly avoid, as we must trust the wisdom of the market. Makes sense?

If BYD has continued to make new highs, while fund flows have also benefited Chinese equities, then why hasn’t Nio emerged from its 52-week lows, as it re-tested support levels last seen in April last year? In any case, I believe you can clearly glean that the upward spikes (July 2023, September 2024, March 2025) that saw a rush in buying was probably due to short covering as such momentum surges are not consistent with slow and steady buying accumulation.

With NIO’s short interest ratio having fallen to about 9.4% in mid-March, I believe investors have likely taken the opportunity to cover some of their bearish positions, as NIO hovers over the $3.8 support level that saw dip-buying over the past one year.

So, some of you may be enticed to nibble or even dip your toes into NIO’s oversold zones and bet on a possibly quick turnaround. Yet, I must caution you that NIO’s near term catalysts seem lacking (especially when it needs to deliver a big result in Q2 to make sure it remains on track for its 440K guidance). With BYD making big strides and its competitors closing in, I think the onus is for management to deliver before buying momentum could return with more conviction.

And even if it does bottom out, betting on a downtrending stock doesn’t seem like the smartest thing to do, when the correction in the US market (and some may say impending bear market) has clearly taken out quite a bit of the froth, as valuations have also normalized, as I presented in my recent market outlook article.

In other words, why try bet your portfolio on a clearly struggling company and stock, when we have a multitude of opportunities to pick from back home? You get my drift.

Rating: Maintain Hold.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: TCW Emerging Markets Local Currency Income Fund Q4 2024 Commentary
data.description:
Performance and Positioning 1

The TCW Emerging Markets Local Currency Income Fund, I Class (“Fund’) returned -7.64% for the fourth quarter of 2024, underperforming the JP Morgan Global Bond Index Emerging Markets Global Diversified (“GBI-EM GD”) return of -6.98% by 66bps. For the year 2024, the Fund returned -3.66%, underperforming the benchmark return of -2.38% by 128bps.

During the fourth quarter of 2024, Emerging Markets local currency fixed income was impacted by a hawkish turn in the Federal Reserve’s outlook notwithstanding the 25bps rate cut in December 2024. Additionally, Emerging Market currencies weakened as the US dollar strengthened (DXY: +7.65% in 4Q 2024) on the back of continued U.S. exceptionalism.

Relative underperformance for the fourth quarter was primarily driven by 1) overweight positioning in Brazil as the local curve repriced and the central bank hiked policy rate by 150bps, 2) overweight positioning in Indonesia as both the external environment and domestic drivers turned unfavorable for monetary policy easing, 3) overweight positioning in South Africa as the South African Rand weakened -8.36% relative to the US dollar, underperforming on a real effective exchange rate basis, 4) relative value FX trade of long JPY / short CNH, 5) CTD overweight in Czech Republic as bond yields widened out with a selloff in core European rates, and 6) a relative value trade of overweight in Romania against an underweight in Poland which hurt as local bonds in Romania sold off on election uncertainty.

On the other hand, relative performance benefited from idiosyncratic trades, including 1) off-index exposure to Nigeria as improvement in fiscal outlook following an external debt raise of $2.2bn supported FX market recovery, 2) allocation and selection in Turkey as the Turkish Lira remained supported on a real effective basis even as the central bank eased policy rates, and 3) off-index exposure to Egypt where structural reforms are underway and strong international support helped to mitigate financial risks from geopolitical conflicts in the region. Our underweight positioning in Thailand also benefitted relative performance for the period.

During the quarter, we added exposure in South Africa, Romania, Nigeria and Egypt while reducing exposure in Brazil, Poland, Mexico and Indonesia. Our overweight in South Africa is set against an underweight in Mexico based on our relative value view of potentially differentiated outcomes resulting from changes in the U.S. trade policy. Since year end, we have reversed our relative value trade in Romania versus Poland and are now neutral to both names.

Relative underperformance for the year was primarily driven by 1) overweight positioning in Brazil as the local curve repriced, the central bank initiated monetary policy tightening and the currency was one of the top underperformers (weakened -21.4% vs. US dollar), 2) underweight positioning in China and Malaysia as their currencies rallied strongly in the third quarter when the Federal Reserve initiated monetary policy easing, 3) FX and rates exposure in Mexico, South Korea and Indonesia as both the external environment and domestic drivers turned unfavorable.

On the other hand, relative performance benefited from idiosyncratic trades, including 1) overweight positioning in Egypt and Turkey as both markets benefited from foreign flows and investor interest following structural reforms, 2) a relative value overweight in India relative to China earlier in the year, and 3) off-index exposure in Nigeria based on our view of the potential for currency appreciation following the aggressive depreciation earlier this year and driven by reforms expected to restore financial market stability, coupled with tighter monetary policy.

As of year-end, our top overweights were South Africa and off-index positions in Egypt and Nigeria while our top underweights were China and Mexico which are more likely to experience volatility as President-elect Trump assumes office. We continue to emphasize idiosyncratic trades and relative value opportunities given our expectation of on-going dollar strength in the short term.

Investment Environment/Outlook

2024 was characterized by a number of market-moving events – stronger-than-expected U.S. growth leading to the unwind of Federal Reserve (Fed) rate cut expectations, a spike in volatility due to a large unwind in JPY-funded carry trades, unexpected election outcomes and elevated geopolitical risk. Still, Emerging Markets (EM) sovereign dollar-denominated debt returned 6.54% in 2024, driven in particular by a 13% rally in high yield (including a 50% return for C-rated sovereigns), reflecting a combination of macro stability, fiscal consolidation, financial support from the IMF or FDI and/or progress on debt restructurings. In fact, EM high yield (HY) sovereigns and corporates were among the top-performing asset classes globally. On the other hand, EM local currency underperformed, down 2.38%, driven by continued U.S. exceptionalism and expectations of tariffs under the incoming Trump administration.

Supportive EM fundamentals and financial resilience anchor our long-term constructive view of the asset class. During the COVID-19 pandemic, Emerging Markets followed a more fiscally conservative approach by necessity, thereby addressing defaults early on. Disinflation trends in EM have been stronger than in Developed Markets (DM) and we anticipate the disinflation process to continue, in part supported by lower commodity prices. Moreover, in EM, current account deficits and external borrowing needs to appear moderate. The IMF is engaged with countries representing over a quarter of the EM sovereign debt index, which helps drive a continuation of conservative fiscal and monetary policies at the lower end of the credit spectrum and the availability of financing in the absence of market access.

We anticipate growth in EM to be relatively stable in 2025, with the EM-DM growth differential remaining at the wider end of the range over the last ten years. We forecast somewhat lower Chinese growth in 2025 versus 2024, as lower policy rates and stimulus have had limited impact on driving consumer demand. While there is elevated uncertainty around the sequencing and magnitude of trade policy, increased fiscal spending is likely to be used as a counter measure against higher tariffs from the U.S. In Latin America, we anticipate that most countries will approach their growth potential and inflation targets by mid- 2026, absent significant external shocks. We forecast stronger CEE GDP growth in 2025, particularly as fiscal policy generally remains stimulative across the region and inflation converges towards targets.

As for monetary policy, Developed Market rates have been restrictive with periods of elevated volatility as investors fear that data-dependent DM central banks are falling behind the curve. The easing bias in DM should provide room for EM central banks to ease policy rates further during 2025, although the Fed’s increased caution and other exogenous factors (e.g., U.S. tariffs, geopolitical tensions) can still limit the pace of cuts in EM.

From a valuation perspective, while absolute credit spreads are at the tighter end of historical ranges, we continue to see value both on an all-in-yield basis and relative to U.S. credit. Spread differentials between EM investment grade (IG) and U.S. IG are on the tighter end of the historical range for the past decade, but on an absolute basis, EM IG trades approximately 45bps wide to US IG. EM HY yields of 10.14% are in the 74th percentile versus history and the spread differential between EM HY sovereigns (559bps) and U.S. HY (287bps) is in the 74th percentile over the past decade. While EM dedicated funds have experienced over $140bn of outflows in aggregate the last three years, demand from cross-over and global investors has been strong, likely demonstrating the value in Emerging Markets relative to other fixed income alternatives.

In our total return portfolios, we continue to overweight high yield relative to investment grade, focusing on high yield idiosyncratic sovereigns such as Egypt, Gabon, Senegal and Turkey. We also see value in select sovereigns such as Brazil and Colombia that have underperformed this year and are more shielded from trade tariffs. We are currently overweight Africa, Latin America, and high yield Europe, funded with a significant underweight to Asia. Additionally, we are maintaining a tactical allocation to EM corporates as this sector tends to outperform in periods of market volatility given higher spreads and shorter durations relative to EM sovereigns. In addition, EM corporate leverage is near the lowest levels in a decade and well below U.S. peers.

We currently have no local currency exposure in our total return portfolios given the strong dollar environment although we continue to look for opportunities at the country level for potential divergence between dollar and local currency returns. US dollar strength remains supported by strong relative growth and higher market interest rates than most DM and a large swath of EM. The USD (i.e., DXY) is within 4% of its 20-year highs, but sustained weakness will require both Fed cuts and stronger growth in the rest of the world in our view. That said, there are risks to continued USD strength, including U.S. fiscal policy excess; possible political pressure on the Fed to ease monetary policy; President-elect Trump’s stated desire to weaken the dollar (albeit without clear policy proposals to date); heavy positioning (global investors own a lot of U.S. assets); the possibility of faster growth in Europe (e.g. the new German government easing fiscal policy, a sustained peace deal in the Russia-Ukraine war); or a growth policy stimulus bazooka from China.

In our local currency portfolios, we continue to emphasize idiosyncratic trades and relative value opportunities given our expectation of on-going dollar strength in the short term.

We believe that risks to Emerging Markets are likely to come more from external than internal factors, including uncertainty around economic and trade policies in the developed world, particularly the United States, and their impact on interest rates, growth and inflation. As the year progresses, there will be more clarity about the extent and sequencing of various policy elements of the new Trump administration and the speed of easing cycles by both the Fed and the ECB. In general, we believe there will be EM winners and losers in this environment and are positioning accordingly.

As for geopolitical risk:

Middle East: The Israel-Hezbollah ceasefire brokered by the U.S. and France and the ousting of the Assad regime from Syria have increased prospects for longer-term de-escalation. Settlement of the conflict would bode well for the region, and in particular for Lebanon, with market optimism for debt restructuring following the formation of a government. That said, a desperate reaction from Iran’s leadership in the wake of recent defeat, as well as President-elect Trump’s signaling of his intention to renew “maximum pressure on Iran”, remains a tail risk scenario. The new Trump Administration’s ultra-hawkish views towards Iran could also embolden Israeli Prime Minister Netanyahu to disregard international pressure and strike Iran directly.

Russia/Ukraine: Many market participants assume that the incoming Trump Administration will pull back financial and military support from Ukraine in an attempt to force Kiev to the negotiating table with Moscow. However, it is not clear what Trump can offer Putin who retains maximalist objectives in Ukraine and Russia and could try to seize this opportunity to try and go much bigger against Ukraine.

South China Sea Tensions: We are monitoring the potential spillover risk of elevated South China Sea tensions into direct confrontation between China’s Navy and Air Force and Taiwan, the U.S., and Washington’s other Asia-Pacific allies. We do not believe any of these parties want to see an escalation in hostilities in the near term, but there is always the possibility for mistakes leading to unintended consequences.

Sincerely,

Penelope D. Foley, Group Managing Director Emerging Markets

David I. Robbins, Group Managing Director Emerging Markets

Alex Stanojevic, Group Managing Director Emerging Markets

Corporate– Of or relating to a bond issued by a corporation as opposed to a bond issued by the U.S. Treasury, a non-U.S. government or a municipality. COVID-19 (Coronavirus disease)– is an infectious disease caused by the SARS-CoV-2 virus. Credit– A contractual agreement in which a borrower receives something of value now and agrees to repay the lender at a later date, generally with interest. Credit also refers to the creditworthiness or credit history of an individual or company. Currency– A generally accepted form of money, including coins and paper notes, which is issued by a government and circulated within an economy. Default– The failure to promptly pay interest or principal when due. Developed Markets (DM)– A country with a relatively high level of economic growth and security. Disinflation– A slowing in the rate of price inflation. Duration– A measure of the sensitivity of the price (the value of principal) of a fixed-income investment to a change in interest rates. Duration is expressed as a number of years. Rising interest rates mean falling bond prices, while declining interest rates mean rising bond prices. DXY (U.S. Dollar Index)– An index (or measure) of the value of the United States dollar relative to a basket of foreign currencies. ECB (European Central Bank)– The European Central Bank (ECB) is the central bank responsible for monetary policy of the European Union (EU) member countries that have adopted the euro currency. This currency union is known as the eurozone and currently includes 19 countries. The ECB’s primary objective is price stability in the euro area. Emerging Market (EM)– A country that has some characteristics of a developed market but is not a developed market. This includes countries that may be developed markets in the future or were in the past. FDI (Foreign Direct Investment)– An investment made by a company or entity based in one country, into a company or entity based in another country. Federal Reserve (the Fed)– The central bank of the United States which regulates the U.S. monetary and financial system. GDP (Gross Domestic Product)– The market value of all final goods and services produced within a country in a given period of time. Growth– A diversified portfolio of stocks that has capital appreciation as its primary goal, with little or no dividend payouts. Portfolio companies would mainly consist of companies with above-average growth in earnings that reinvest their earnings into expansion, acquisitions, and/or research and development. Hawkishness– Generally favoring relatively high interest rates in order to keep inflation in check. High Yield– A bond that is rated below investment grade. IMF (International Monetary Fund)– An international organization created for the purpose of promoting global monetary and exchange rate stability, facilitating the expansion and balanced growth of international trade and assisting in the establishment of a multilateral system of payments for current transactions. Inflation– A condition of a rise in the general level of prices of goods and services in an economy over a period of time. Investment Grade (IG)– A bond with a rating of Baa or higher from Moody’s Investors Service, and a rating of BBB or higher from Standard & Poor’s. JP Morgan EMBI Global Diversified Index (EMBI GD)– A market capitalization-weighted total return index of

U.S. dollar-denominated Brady bonds, loans, and Eurobond instruments traded in emerging markets. JPY– Japanese Yen. Local Currency– The most common form of currency used in a country. This usually encompasses the national currency of the country. Monetary Policy– The actions of a central bank, currency board or other regulatory committee that determine the size and rate of growth of the money supply, which in turn affects interest rates. Mutual Funds– An investment vehicle that is made up of a pool of funds collected from many investors for the purpose of investing in securities such as stocks, bonds, money-market instruments and similar assets. Mutual funds are operated by money managers, who invest the fund’s capital and attempt to produce capital gains and income for the fund’s investors. A mutual fund’s portfolio is structured and maintained to match the investment objectives stated in its prospectus. Outperform– Outperform is when an investment is expected to perform better than the return generated by a particular index or the overall market. Since the performance of many investments is compared to a benchmark index, outperform refers to generating a higher return than a particular benchmark over time. Outperform also refers to an analyst’s rating on a security, and outperform is a better rating than neutral and worse than a strong buy recommendation. Overweight– A condition where the portfolio exposure to a given asset class (or risk measure) exceeds that of the benchmark index. S&P 500– The S&P 500 Index, or Standard & Poor’s 500 Index, is a market-capitalization-weighted index of 500 leading publicly traded companies in the U.S. The index actually has 503 components because three of them have two share classes listed. Sovereign– A national government within a given country. Spread– The difference between the bid and the ask price of a security or asset. Stimulus– Something that causes a reaction; an incentive. Tariff– A tax imposed on imported goods and services. Total Return– The rate of return on a security, including income from dividends and interest, as well as appreciation or depreciation in the price of the security, over a given time period of time. Trump – Current Republican nominee for U.S. President, Donald J. Trump. Underperform– When an investment is underperforming, it is not keeping pace with other securities or indices. Underweight– A condition where a portfolio does not hold a sufficient amount of a particular security when compared to the security’s weight in the underlying benchmark portfolio. USD (United States Dollar)– Abbreviated as USD, the dollar is the currency for the United States of America. Valuations– The process of determining the current worth of an asset or company. There are many techniques that can be used to determine value, some are subjective and others are objective. Volatility– A measure of the risk of price moves for a security calculated from the standard deviation of day-to-day logarithmic historical price changes. Yield– The income return on an investment. This refers to the interest or dividends received from a security and is usually expressed annually as a percentage based on the investment’s cost, its current market value or its face value.

For more information about the Fund call us at 800 386 3829.

Visit our web site for a full menu of products and services at TCW.com.

The TCW Funds are distributed by TCW Funds Distributors LLC

Original Post
