data.title: Strawberry Fields - One Of The Exceptions To My Rule About Small REITs
data.description:
Dear readers/followers,

Strawberry Fields (NYSE:STRW) is a REIT that I have looked at before, but never reviewed. I got my eyes on it when it was first brought to me from another investor, and I went ahead and purchased a small position with a long time frame.

That position has outperformed since then. However, because it was and still is small, I have never covered it - it's too small to be of note. Especially when the company got more expensive, I didn't think there was much to "say".

Well, now I believe I can look at the company in the long term, and I want to wait for when there is a valuation opportunity in the REIT, and when there is, I'd want to "pounce". I say this because the fundamental improvements and the clarity I believe are available in the business here make the company a very interesting play.

I believe if a dip in valuation were to happen, this would (depending on if there isn't some sort of massive fundamental issue) make the company one of the most interesting buying potentials out there - at least if you can get over the company's very small market cap, which this point doesn't even reach a billion.

Let's see what we have here.

Strawberry fields - where things are today

The company became much more relevant to me after it uplisted from the OTC market to the New York Stock Exchange. That happened in 2023, and that's also when I went ahead and bought it.

The company has a long history, much longer than the ticker, over 20 years ago when the current CEO and Chairman started buying up skilled nursing facilities in one specific State, Indiana. This, in turn, led to the spinning out of a propco, which it did with 33 facilities in the states of Indiana and Illinois.

Strawberry Fields, despite being a very small operator comparatively, comes with exceptional expertise in the field, owning and leasing about 130 facilities in 11 US states. This also means that it's an American company. The recent valuation upswing has really enhanced its RoR but has also impacted its yield. From a 5%+ dividend yield, we're now down to below 5%, with a 2024 AFFO of around $55.8M for the full year, which, based on the current number of shares comes to a 2024 AFFO of $1.1/share, which is almost a 10% increase. The same is true for AEBITDA, which grew as well.

From a safety and liquidity profile, Strawberry Fields has debt, but it's at low interest, fixed, and comes at maturities of over 20 years with a weighted interest rate on average at below 4%. One of the things the company actually does is take on debt outside of the US, such as its recent bond, which was Israel-based.

Strawberry Fields is an income-focused REIT, that has almost 15,000 beds in its portfolio, with a triple-net lease structure and annual rent escalators of about 1-3%. This doesn't make it special - a lot of operators do exactly this or operations similar to it.

But usually investing in small players carries bigger risks in terms of fundamentals. I do not consider that necessarily to be the case for this company. 100% of rent was collected in the latest period, and the company has a net debt leverage ratio of less than 52%, a well-filled acquisition pipeline, and good over 1.5x TTM rent coverage on the basis of EBITDARM. While there are REITs where coverage and fundamentals is better, their growth trajectory is also very different than this company.

What is further positive is that the company lacks the typical east or west-coast exposure, but instead focuses on the mid and south areas, with heavy exposures in the following states.

Because of this, an argument can be made that this company offers geographic diversification as well as good fundamentals.

The company is stewarded by a team of professionals with typical experience in the industry of several decades, with top management having served in positions, such as the CEO, since the very founding of the company. The advantage of these "small" REITS is that they are quite often overlooked and thought to be much riskier than the average, often for no reason. While an overconcentrated REIT does, of course, come with this risk, I do not view Strawberry Fields as overconcentrated in every single way, even if the company does have some tenant concentration risks.

How?

Because the leases of 119 properties are leased to over 125 operators across 11 states. The company has, from the get-go, unlike other REITs, not seen an over-concentration in any way. The company also ensures that any tenant it has, in turn, has support from top-tier consulting groups, which are experienced in the way of maintaining profitability while giving good care.

While tenants using consulting groups is in no way unique, the way the company uses them here, I have not seen this explicitly in any other REIT - and that gives me further confidence in the company's operating model because STRW has not seen any sort of payment shortfall that would be cause for concern during what I can find in the entirety of its operating history. The risk that remains here is therefore the concentration risk, where Infinity Healthcare is the largest tenant, accounting for a massive majority of the company's ABR.

The arguments for an operator in this field write themselves. It's moaty, with high barriers, a growing demand, subsidies from the federal level, and low new supply, as seen above. The problem is that some of these advantages, such as Medicare and Medicaid are now subject to higher uncertainty than before.

It's that which I believe may, in the short to medium term, present an interesting opportunity to invest in this company and companies like it. While the administration may curtail spending, I have a very conservative view on the potential that they're going to cut spending in a way that would make the business model unworkable.

The REIT meanwhile, has shown great growth. AFFO is up 12.6% CAGR since 2019 and adjusted EBITDA at more than 8% per year since the same. Average base rent, or ABR, is up 7.5% CAGR with a significant bump last year, and the company has continued to grow its assets since then as well. In terms of AFFO, the company is conservative in a way that very few REITs are because as of 2024, only 49.5% of AFFO goes to the dividend, leaving ample room for the company to continue growing.

The things and metrics I would instead keep an eye on - because the size, company payout, and the like are safe, the company has an average facility occupancy of sub-70%, and over 76% of the payors are based on Medicaid.

A cut to this program would be, in no uncertain terms, and regardless of your political view, short-term disastrous. In Missouri, over 20% of the population is reliant on Medicaid, and even conservative politicians don't want to see cuts to the program (Source). Of course, Medicaid coverage varies widely by both states and by benefits and terms of eligibility.

The point is that any potential cut could and likely would impact patients, and in turn tenant-operators such as the ones we have in STRW. This is a risk to keep an eye on, and I am honestly somewhat surprised that the market hasn't punished the company for this. The way that I interpret this is that any significant cuts are very unlikely, as by the way, Mr. Donald Trump, the current president, has also said.

Let me show you the valuation and the potential that makes me interested here, given that the operational specifics are good enough, in my view, to consider for an investment.

Strawberry fields - The valuation needs to reflect the risks - but the upside is here

So, I would say that the two major risks to STRW are one, tenant concentration, and two, Medicaid exposure. The second is one that is general to all of the companies in this field, and the former is one that is so-so. I own REITs that have it, but I try to discount for it, just as I will try to discount for here as well.

Basic valuation fundamentals, STRW trades at $11.4/share, which comes to about 10.5x to AFFO. This is "okay". It's higher than where I would ideally like it to be, given my return hurdle. What it means is that STRW at a forecast basis of about 15x currently is forecasted to generate over 25% annualized and triple-digits until 2027E.

So why am I not putting $100k to work?

Because obviously, this is on the basis of no discount - and I believe the company needs to be discounted a bit. At 15x P/AFFO, you're essentially putting STRW at a level close to O, and STRW is not O. Historical valuation multiples have put the company at around 8-10x p/AFFO, and this is more where I believe it should be, given its current size and risk parameters. Forecasting at the lower end of that spectrum, around 8x, gives us instead a sub-9% annualized P/AFFO, which obviously is insufficient.

At around 10x, it looks better, and it's barely grazing that 15% hurdle per year. But since it only "hits" that level at 10x P/AFFO, I consider this to be insufficient.

I believe STRW can become a spectacular investment at the right price. If it were to trade at single digits, I would see it possible to invest at a higher, 16-20% annualized RoR, and at an attractive yield of close to 5%. I would then increase my current, small position (which has nonetheless provided an excellent return).

Outside of a single-digit share price, though, I would say things are a bit too speculative here still. Much of the upside comes from a current forecasted 20% AFFO growth in 2027E, which has no historical backing of accuracy whatsoever, and while I don't doubt management's skill, I think anyone claiming to currently see 2-3 years in the future should consider more than just a bit of "salt". Especially given that their main client at over 50% of ABR is a private company, meaning that information available is really quite limited.

For this reason, I say "Hold" here, but I could consider it as a "BUY" at a slightly better valuation.

My thesis for the stock is now as follows.

Thesis

  • Strawberry REIT is an attractive skilled nursing REIT under superb management with a great upside at the right sort of valuation - unfortunately, we currently don't have the "right" valuation to make it work.
  • I have a small in this somewhat thinly traded REIT, and one I intend to keep unless the company goes above $14/share. If that were to happen, I would then probably rotate my stake.
  • At this point, I would consider the company a "HOLD" interesting only in the case of the company dropping below around $10/share.

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them (italicized).

  • This company is overall qualitative.
  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative & well-run.
  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.
  • This company is currently cheap.
  • This company has a realistic upside based on earnings growth or multiple expansion/reversion.

The company fulfills 3 out of 3 quality indicators, and 0 out of 2 valuation indicators. That means 3/5 "stars" here, which typically isn't enough. Based on this, and a valuation target of $10/share where I see a risk/reward-adjusted upside that's attractive enough for me. I give the company a "Hold" rating as of April 2025

data.title: BlackRock TCP: Still Too Speculative After The Dividend Cut
data.description:
In my previous piece on BlackRock TCP Capital Corp (NASDAQ:TCPC) I compared it with Oaktree Specialty Lending (NASDAQ:OCSL) to determine which one would cut its dividend first.

My conclusion was that OCSL would do it first and then shortly after it we would see an announcement for a dividend cut by TCPC's management.

This turned out to be correct - both in terms of the sequence and the fact that the dividends will be lower for 2025.

In TCPC's case investors experienced a ~26% base dividend cut, which has now lead a quarterly base dividend amount of $0.25 per share.

As a result of this, TCPC's FWD yield now stands at 13.7%, which is actually still materially above the sector median of 11.6%.

At the same time the P/NAV stands at 0.83x, which, theoretically, offers a very enticing entry point for any BDC investor. In fact, by having this kind of a P/NAV multiple, TCPC ranks as ninth cheapest BDC.

All in all, the setup for making an investment move in TCPC looks optically interesting or at least as something that is worth considering.

So, let's take a look at TCPC to see whether there is a decent investment case.

Thesis review

If we start with the most important metric, which measures the level of BDC earnings generation - adjusted NII per share - we will have to admit that TCPC has finally managed to halt the decline that was in place starting from mid 2023. In Q4 2024, TCPC registered an adjusted NII per share of $0.36, which was perfectly in line with the figure achieved in Q3 2024.

Yet, here is the thing.

First, the top-line still contracted heavily. It dropped by ~15% on a quarter-to-quarter basis as it was driven by the previous loan write-offs and lower yields.

Second, the only reason why the adjusted NII per share remained flat is related to the cancelation of incentive fees. Management has decided to avoid them until a specific total return hurdle rate is met.

Third, a decent part of the income that was generated in Q4 stemmed from one-time items, most notably from loan prepayments, which come at a cost (and thus TCPC income) for the borrowers.

I think you will agree with me that the Q4 adjusted NII per share has to be taken with a grain of salt.

Now, in terms of the future adjusted NII per share generation, the things become even less straightforward.

Theoretically, we could make a realistic assumption that there will not be any material expenses coming from the emergence of incentive fees as it is unlikely that TCPC manages to materially increase the earnings.

However, we have to also factor in the following items:

  • TCPC's debt to equity is 1.27x, which is above sector average and an indication of a largely exhausted financial capacity to expand the portfolio size and thus capture incremental spreads.
  • TCPC ended Q4 2024 with yet another negative net funding statistic, which means that in Q1 2025 there will be a smaller portfolio base from which to generate NII.
  • In February, 2026, TCPC will have to deal with a refinancing of $325 million note that has been assumed at 2.85%, which is way below the market-level borrowing rate for BDCs (i.e., it is closer to ~7%). The interest rates might drop, but it is hard to imagine how TCPC could avoid the damage of higher borrowing costs after the debt rollover.
  • The current portfolio yield for TCPC is 12.4%, which has dropped from 13.4% in Q3 2024. The new investments had a weighted average yield of 10.8%, which is a better reflection of the market-level rates. This means two things: 1) TCPC is subject to unfavorable debt repricings, and 2) the adjusted NII per share result will, most likely, continue to decline.

Apart from this, another nuance to take into account is the portfolio quality angle. For example, while Q4 adjusted NII per share covered the previous dividends, the net unrealized loss of $0.79 per share drove down the NAV materially. The NAV per share decreased from $10.11 in Q3 to $9.23 now. Plus, the non-accrual rate (on a FV basis) is still quite elevated at ~5.6%. Currently, 8.5% of TCPC FV consists of restructured loans that have turned into non-performing equity positions. It would be only prudent to assume that TCPC does not succeed in monetizing it (at least when it comes to any material chunks of it).

The bottom line

In a nutshell, TCPC looks great on the surface, but by peeling the onion back there is a different conclusion.

The current base dividend coverage after the cut and assuming Q4 2024 adjusted NII per share is 144%. This is very high. In addition, the P/NAV of 0.83x offers a decent discount at which to buy TCPC's NAV.

However, as I elaborated above, there are many factors that have almost a guaranteed negative impact on TCPC's future adjusted NII per share generation. And if TCPC gets hit again by incremental non-accruals, I am afraid that management will have to revisit the dividend yet gain.

Given these kinds of dynamics and considering the prevailing macro environment, where defense has become increasingly important, I would avoid making a speculative investment in TCPC.

data.title: LVHI: Global Exposure That Delivers Less Volatility
data.description:
Overview

Now that the first quarter of 2025 is over, the S&P 500 (SPX) has underperformed global stocks by over 9%. In the chance of a shift in sentiment related to US markets, I was looking for the most efficient way to capitalize on international markets. Over 90% of my portfolio is concentrated to US markets, and I wanted to diversify a bit. I wanted to also gain global exposure from a fund that also provided a sizable dividend income. I believe that Franklin International Low Volatility High Dividend Index ETF (BATS:LVHI) matches the criteria that I was searching for, and I wanted to share some insights on the fund in case any readers also wanted to diversify their portfolio to global markets.

Looking at the performance over the last twelve months, LVHI's price increased by about 8.4%. When including all dividends paid to shareholders, the total return jumps up to about 14.5% over the same period. Following the recent market decline, LVHI offers a starting dividend yield of about 5%. This makes it an attractive choice for investors looking to create a sizable income stream over time. However, the fund hasn't really demonstrated a consistent dividend growth trend over time, so my expectations for dividend growth are limited.

LVHI has a public inception dating back to 2016 so we have nearly a decade worth of performance history to reference. LVHI is actively managed by Franklin Templeton and has an expense ratio of 0.40%. With ongoing market volatility from President Trump's ongoing tariff proposals, a questionable interest rate outlook, and higher than anticipated inflationary data, investors are looking for a place to navigate these conditions with minimal stress. The fund searches for holdings that have demonstrated a high dividend yield but also low volatility related to price and earnings. Therefore, LVHI is a great option for investors seeking better capital preservation through market declines. So let's start by taking a look at the underlying strategies that LVHI implements to build out its portfolio.

Fund Strategy

According to the latest fund breakdown, LVHI has net assets totaling $2.33B spread across 140 different holdings. What makes LVHI unique is that all of the fund's assets are spread across different regions of the world, making it possible to instantly add some global diversification to your portfolio. We can see that Japan makes up the largest geographic allocation at 14.54%. This is followed by varying amounts of weight allocated across different regions of Europe, Asia, and Australia. In fact, less than 0.4% of assets within LVHI are allocated within USA markets.

From a sector breakdown, we can see that Financials make up the largest bulk of the fund at 23.28%. This is followed by weight to investments in the Energy and Materials sectors, accounting for 15.11% and 10.40% respectively. The combination of these three sectors makes up over 48% of net assets, so we can assume that these will have the greatest impact on LVHI's daily price change and total return potential.

However, it's worth noting that LVHI lacks a meaningful exposure to the technology sector. Information technology only accounts for 1.72% of the fund, which means that LVHI will likely miss out on any of the market growth related to innovations within the AI sector. As companies start to implement different AI technologies within their products and services, this may eventually materialize into growth of the tech sector. However, the low allocation here makes sense since most of the notable innovations are being led by US companies.

Looking at the top ten holdings, we can see that Nippon Telegraph and Telephone Corp. (OTCPK:NTTYY) is the largest holding at 2.6%. This is followed by varying weights in other notable companies such as GSK plc (GSK), Roche Holding (OTCQX:RHHBY), and Rio Tinto Group (RIO). The majority of the holdings are large-cap companies. The fund doesn't deploy any fancy option writing strategies or leverage, so the holdings are straightforward.

Resilience

One of the main appeals to sprinkling in some global exposure to your portfolio is better resilience against downturns in US indexes, such as the S&P 500 or Nasdaq. On a YTD basis, US indexes have declined as investors don't seem to favor the ongoing tariff proposals. As a result, the SPDR S&P 500 ETF (SPY) has a total loss of 4.27%, while the Invesco QQQ Trust (QQQ) has a total loss of 8.14%. In comparison, LVHI has actually maintained a positive total return of 6.48% over the same period.

If you wanted to see a longer time horizon as another example, we don't have to look any further than the year of 2022. Market indexes declined throughout 2022 due to the Fed's rate shock, as the aggressively hiked interest rates to combat record-breaking inflation levels. SPY reported a total loss of 18.17% for the year, while QQQ saw a total loss over 32.5%. In comparison, LVHI saw a positive total return of 3.82% over the same period.

Goldman Sachs released a report that estimated the average annual return of the S&P 500 to fall to 3%. This is a major decrease from the +10% average annual returns that investors have grown accustomed to over the last decade. The short summary of this report is that analysts believe it's simply unsustainable for these technology companies to continue reporting massive double-digit sales growth as they have over the past decade. Referencing statistical data, the report mentions that only 11% of S&P 500 companies have been able to sustain +10% sales growth for 10 consecutive years. Assuming a similar outlook, this means that the majority of the Mag 7 companies are likely to see slower growth eventually.

While we don't have a crystal ball to confirm whether or not an outlook like this is correct, it does bring up some concerns about a possible shift in the market. If growth of the technology sector slows, it could cause investor capital to rotate out of the sector in search for better opportunities. Portions of this capital could flow into different parts of the global market and different sectors that would benefit LVHI.

Dividend Comparison

As of the most recently declared quarterly dividend of $0.13 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 5%. Despite the higher starting yield, the dividend payouts are highly variable, with Q4's payout being the largest of the year. Looking back over the dividend history, we can also see an inconsistent annual payout amount over the last decade. A more consistent dividend payout rate is definitely an improvement that the fund can make.

It's also worth mentioning that the distributions aren't typically classified as qualified dividends. The distribution history indicates that dividends are funded by a combination of long-term, short-term, and income. Therefore, dividends received from LVHI will have tax implications that investors should consider. Since the Q4 payout is historically the largest, this will have the largest tax burden for investors. Based on your specific income needs, amount of capital invested, and current tax bracket, LVHI may be best utilized within a tax advantaged account. However, I would consult with a tax professional to determine what's best for your specific situation.

I was curious as to how LVHI performed against a more well-known international dividend ETF like Vanguard International High Dividend Yield Index Fund ETF (VYMI). While VYMI's approach is slightly different, the fund shares a lot of similar holdings and a familiar sector breakdown. One of the main differences is that VYMI has nearly 1,500 holdings within, making it a much more diverse fund for investors looking to get a wider range of exposure. From a total return perspective, we can see that LVHI has outperformed VYMI since its inception.

However, VYMI has a much better dividend growth and payout consistency compared to LVHI. For instance, VYMI has been able to increase dividends at a CAGR (compound annual growth rate) of 5.37% over the last five years. LVHI has actually experienced a negative dividend CAGR of 4.28% over the same five-year period. So VYMI may be a better choice for investors that want to prioritize a better dividend growth rate going forward. VYMI's better dividend growth could be the result of a greater amount of underlying holdings. I did previously release an article on VYMI if you are interested in learning more about an alternative fund.

Weakness

A clear weakness of these international funds is the underperformance against their US-counterparts. US equities have historically outpaced the growth of international stocks, and the performance shows when comparing the total return of these US alternatives. I randomly selected peer dividend ETFs that put an emphasis on a high dividend yield, including:

  • Amplify CWP Enhanced Dividend Income ETF (DIVO)
  • Vanguard High Dividend Yield Index Fund (VYM)
  • Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD)
  • Fidelity High Dividend ETF (FDVV)

As we can see below, LVHI underperforms all of the mentioned peers in total return. However, the point of LVHI is to be used as an accompanying fund to an already diverse portfolio, rather than a standalone ETF.

Additionally, the lack of technology exposure is something that many of these dividend funds have an issue with. Technology companies aren't typically known for their prioritization on paying shareholders dividends. Therefore, these tend to outperform more traditional ETFs that have a more sizable exposure to the technology sector. The most notable source of growth for the tech sector would be the increasing demand for within the Artificial Intelligence space. Precedence Research estimates that the AI market can grow at a CAGR of 19.20% through 2034. When these new innovations start to materialize into higher valuations, LVHI will likely be left behind.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I rate LVHI as a buy due to the ability to offset the volatility experienced in US indexes. The global exposure has proven to deliver better capital preservation and less volatility through market declines. This makes LVHI a great accompanying fund to help navigate periods of uncertainty in the markets. Despite the fund having an inconsistent dividend history, the higher starting yield can also make LVHI a great option for investors seeking to diversify their dividend income stream outside of US equities. However, investors should consider the potential tax implications of the dividends received. LVHI provides a wide range of exposure to holdings that are based throughout Asia, Europe, and Australia.

data.title: Dividends By The Numbers In March 2025 And Q1 2025
data.description:
U.S. stock market dividends turned in a mostly negative performance in March 2025.

On the positive side of the ledger, March 2025's 16 dividend decreases came in below the 23 recorded in February 2025. Meanwhile, the year-over-year change in dividend reductions was little changed, increasing by just one over March 2024's total.

However, the change in the number of favorable changes like dividend increases, extra dividends and resumed dividends was decidedly negative. Combined, these three categories added to 141 favorable dividend actions for March 2025, which is 59 less than the 200 reported a year earlier.

This significant year-over-year reduction in favorable dividend actions combines with the small increase in unfavorable dividend changes to give March 2025 an overall score of -60. The following table presents Standard & Poor's dividend metadata for March 2025. It summarizes how the month's dividend data compares in both Month-over-Month (MoM) and Year-Over-Year (YoY) terms with previously reported data:

The following chart shows how these numbers fit in the longer-running context of dividend increases and decreases since January 2004.

March 2025 represents a continuation of an ongoing negative trend for dividend increases that's been going on since the first quarter of 2023.

Switching to quarterly dividend results, the next chart groups the previous 15 months worth of dividend increase and decrease data into quarters, covering the last five quarters from 2024-Q1 through 2025-Q1. The chart shows 2025-Q1 was more negative for dividend-paying companies in the U.S. stock market than 2024-Q1 was.

The silver lining in this data is that the overall number of monthly dividend decreases remains below the threshold that is consistent with recessionary conditions being present in the U.S. economy.

But with a falling number of favorable dividend actions, how long might that continue?
